questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper reports pre-training on ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M. It also evaluates transfer performance on smaller benchmarks including ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, with 87M parameters. The paper’s reference vision transformer is 86M, but the biggest variant in the provided evidence is 87M.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention inside non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design yields linear computational complexity with respect to image size. Relative position bias is another architectural choice highlighted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper’s architecture is DINO: a simple self-supervised setup with a student network trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, using centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is applied to both Vision Transformers and convnets, with the paper emphasizing ViTs—especially small-patch variants—as particularly effective.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 up to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower SOTA-like values such as PaLI-17B at 90.9 and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The highest reported value in the evidence is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin), while the lowest is 48.5%; no explicit SOTA claims are listed in the evidence.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: Not enough data on ADE20K/mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU) to compare.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The ADE20K mIoU values in the corpus are not consistent: they range from 26.6 to 62.9, a spread of 36.3 points with stddev 7.29. The corpus also contains multiple SOTA claims at different values, including InternImage-H at 62.9, ViT-Adapter-L at 61.5, and SwinV2-G at 59.9, so the reported best numbers are not uniform.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No, the corpus does not fully agree. Some papers treat position embeddings as part of the standard ViT design or say they are needed to add positional information, e.g. ViT says it “adds position embeddings,” and CPVT notes that “positional encodings are commonly employed” because self-attention is permutation-equivariant. But other papers report they are not necessary or have only a small effect: CvT says “Removing position embedding of CvT does not degrade performance,” and ConViT says masking them has a much smaller effect than in DeiT; one study also says removing them only slightly hurts self-supervised ViT performance.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy increased from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. Overall, the trend is up from 2020 to a peak in 2022, followed by a downward trend through 2024.

questions.question: How many papers were published each year in this corpus, and which year had the most?
questions.system_answer: The corpus has 5 papers in 2019, 7 in 2020, 49 in 2021, 21 in 2022, 12 in 2023, 4 in 2024, and 2 in 2025. The highest count is 2021 with 49 papers.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: Average model parameter count increased substantially from 2020 to 2023, rising from ~161M in 2020 to ~4099M in 2023, before dropping in 2024 due to small sample size.
questions.system_answer: The average model parameter count rises sharply from 2020 to 2023, then drops in 2024. It starts at about 161.1M in 2020, increases to 291.0M in 2021 and 780.6M in 2022, peaks at 4,098.8M in 2023, and then falls to 50.0M in 2024.

questions.question: In which year did papers in this corpus first mention self-supervised learning?
questions.system_answer: The first paper in the corpus to mention self-supervised learning appears in 2020. The trend then rises to 4 mentions in 2021, before dropping to 1 mention in both 2022 and 2023.

questions.question: How did the number of papers using COCO change from year to year in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: COCO usage peaked in 2021 with 17 papers, but was not consistently increasing—it dropped from 4 in 2019 to 1 in 2020, peaked at 17 in 2021, then declined to 10 in 2022 and 4 in 2023.
questions.system_answer: The number of papers mentioning COCO started at 4 in 2019, dropped to 1 in 2020, then rose sharply to 17 in 2021. After that, it declined to 10 in 2022, 4 in 2023, and 1 in 2024, so the overall trend from 2019 to 2024 is downward with a spike in 2021.
